target.name: Slate Auto
target.founder: Miles Arnone (Co-founder, CEO of Re:Build Manufacturing), Will Barker (Co-founder, Re:Build), Jeff Wilke (Co-founder, ex-CEO Worldwide Consumer at Amazon)

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $1.4B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Transportation (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Transportation, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Harbinger
private.rationale: Same sector (Transportation), same stage (Series C), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Slate Auto is a Series C transportation company founded by experienced operators including Jeff Wilke (former CEO Worldwide Consumer at Amazon) and Miles Arnone (co-founder of Re:Build Manufacturing). The target sector comprises eight identified private peers, including Rivian, Harbinger, The Boring Company, Einride, and REGENT Craft; no public comparables exist in the narrow peer set.

Stage-progression analysis applies a 1.7x multiplier to the $1.4B total capital raised, yielding $2.4B. This method is applicable given Slate Auto's Series C stage and typical step-up patterns at that funding round. Peer-median triangulation does not apply: while five same-sector peers exist in transportation, the narrow filters (same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier) produced insufficient datapoints—fewer than the three needed for reliable median derivation. Broadening to all-sector peers would introduce unacceptable methodological noise.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $2.0B to $2.7B, centered on $2.4B. This median estimate rests on a single applicable method, limiting conviction. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow-set peer data for triangulation and reliance on stage-progression multiples rather than revenue or growth metrics. The range reflects reasonable bounds around stage-typical step-ups but should be treated as preliminary pending additional market data or company-specific fundamentals.
